elements.sent-2411-001: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance across various machine learning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: Yet the substantial memory footprint of LLMs significantly hinders their deployment.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we improve the accessibility of LLMs through BitMoD\footnote{Code is available at: https://github.com/yc2367/BitMoD-HPCA-25 , an algorithm-hardware co-design solution that enables efficient LLM acceleration at low weight precision.}
elements.sent-2411-004: On the algorithm side, BitMoD introduces fine-grained data type adaptation that uses a different numerical data type to quantize a group of (e.g., 128) weights.
elements.sent-2411-005: Through the careful design of these new data types, BitMoD is able to quantize LLM weights to very low precision (e.g., 4 bits and 3 bits) while maintaining high accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-006: On the hardware side, BitMoD employs a bit-serial processing element to easily support multiple numerical precisions and data types; our hardware design includes two key innovations: First, it employs a unified representation to process different weight data types, thus reducing the hardware cost.
elements.sent-2411-007: Second, it adopts a bit-serial dequantization unit to rescale the per-group partial sum with minimal hardware overhead.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our evaluation on six representative LLMs demonstrates that BitMoD significantly outperforms state-of-the-art LLM quantization and acceleration methods.
elements.sent-2411-009: For discriminative tasks, BitMoD can quantize LLM weights to 4-bit with $<\!0.5\%$ accuracy loss on average.
elements.sent-2411-010: For generative tasks, BitMoD is able to quantize LLM weights to 3-bit while achieving better perplexity than prior LLM quantization scheme.
elements.sent-2411-011: Combining the superior model performance with an efficient accelerator design, BitMoD achieves an average of $1.69\times$ and $1.48\times$ speedups compared to prior LLM accelerators ANT and OliVe, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-012: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved significant breakthroughs in natural language processing tasks~\cite{opt, llama .}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the growth of LLM size and complexity continues to outpace the scaling of compute performance and memory capacity in existing hardware platforms~\cite{AI-mem-wall .}
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, the first generation of the GPT model, introduced in 2018, contains only 117 million parameters, while the second and third generations grew more than $10\times$ and $1000\times$, respectively within two years~\cite{GPT3 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: This rapid increase in size necessitates significant memory capacity for model deployment, hindering their wide adoption, especially in edge scenarios with limited compute and memory resources.
elements.sent-2411-016: For instance, the state-of-the-art (SOTA) open-source LLM family, \text{Llama-3 ~\cite{llama-3 , contains more than 8 billion parameters and requires more than 16GB of memory to store the model weights in 16-bit floating-point (FP16) format, which cannot fit in an edge GPU such as Jetson-TX2 with 8GB memory~\cite{jetson-gpu .}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, designing novel LLM compression algorithms, together with accelerators co-designed for efficient deployment of the compressed models, presents a promising solution to enhancing the accessibility of LLMs on edge devices.
elements.sent-2411-018: Quantization serves as one of the most hardware-efficient methods to mitigate the computation and memory demands of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-019: Generally, there are two types of quantization mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-020: The first one is quantization-aware training (QAT), where retraining is needed to update model weights and quantization parameters (e.g., scaling factors)~\cite{ant, llm-qat .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The second approach is post-training quantization (PTQ), which does not require retraining~\cite{smoothquant, gptq, awq, students, omniquant, olive, quip .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Although QAT can achieve more competitive accuracy than PTQ, the prohibitive cost of retraining LLMs makes it less practical.
elements.sent-2411-023: As a result, PTQ is commonly adopted in existing LLM quantization studies.
elements.sent-2411-024: While some PTQ works quantize both weights and activations into low precision~\cite{smoothquant, omniquant, olive , weight-only quantization can offer a better trade-off between model accuracy and hardware efficiency for edge deployment of LLMs, where weights dominate the memory footprint~\cite{gptq, awq, students, quip .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, existing weight-only quantization works on GPUs suffer from poor computational efficiency since GPUs lack dedicated hardware to perform multiplication between integer weight and floating-point activation.
elements.sent-2411-026: Consequently, these methods must first dequantize the weight to FP16 and rely on the floating-point pipeline for computation.
elements.sent-2411-027: To achieve better computational efficiency for LLMs, a recent accelerator work, FIGNA~\cite{figna , proposes a family of dedicated computing units for mixed-precision arithmetic between integer weights and floating-point activations.}
elements.sent-2411-028: To further unleash the potential of quantization for improved hardware efficiency, several works have proposed algorithm-hardware co-design solutions based on custom low-precision data types~\cite{microscaling, mx-format, ant, olive .}
elements.sent-2411-029: The microscaling format (MX)~\cite{microscaling, mx-format , assigns 8-bit metadata as the shared exponent to a group of low-precision weights.}
elements.sent-2411-030: ANT~\cite{ant introduces a new data type that better adapts to the intra-tensor value distribution, thus reducing the quantization error.}
elements.sent-2411-031: OliVe~\cite{olive proposes an outlier-victim-pair quantization mechanism, where an outlier value with a large magnitude is represented with an ``Adaptive Biased Float" format and can be protected by pruning its adjacent victim value that has a small magnitude.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In this paper, we propose BitMoD\footnote{BitMoD stands for Bit-serial computation with Mixture of Data types.}
elements.sent-2411-033: On the algorithm side, BitMoD exploits the per-group quantization~\cite{group-quant , and modifies low-precision floating-point data types by repurposing the redundant zero value with a special value, which provides the ability to better adapt the data type itself to the numerical distribution of each weight group.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Through careful choice of special values, BitMoD is able to quantize LLM weights to very low precision (e.g., 4-bit and 3-bit) with tiny encoding overhead while maintaining good model accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-035: On the hardware side, BitMoD employs the bit-serial computing paradigm with a unified representation for different low-precision data types to efficiently trade-off weight precision and hardware efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-036: The main contributions of this paper are summarized below:.
elements.sent-2411-037: We propose BitMoD, a hardware-efficient PTQ solution for LLM acceleration.
elements.sent-2411-038: BitMoD introduces new data types that are tailored for per-group weight quantization at 4-bit and 3-bit precision with tiny encoding overhead.
elements.sent-2411-039: We demonstrate that the proposed data types can be seamlessly integrated with other quantization optimization techniques, achieving better model perplexity than SOTA software-only LLM quantization works.
elements.sent-2411-040: We propose an efficient accelerator design for BitMoD, which adopts a unified bit-serial representation for multiple low-precision data types.
elements.sent-2411-041: This effectively reduces the hardware cost to perform computation between low-precision weights and FP16 activations, and trades-off weight precision for improved hardware efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our evaluation on six representative LLMs shows that on average, BitMoD achieves $2.2\times$ speedup and $2.31\times$ better energy efficiency compared to the baseline FP16 accelerator, without loss in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-043: Compared to SOTA accelerators ANT and OliVe, BitMoD achieves an average speedup of $1.69\times$ and $1.48\times$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-044: To demonstrate the importance of LLM weight quantization for edge applications, we profile the total memory access footprint of weight and activation for four representative LLMs running both discriminative and generative tasks with a batch size of 1.
elements.sent-2411-045: For discriminative tasks, the LLM receives an input context and outputs a single token such as in sentiment analysis~\cite{sst2 and multiple-choice question answering~\cite{arc-c .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: For generative tasks, the LLM receive an input context and output multiple tokens.
elements.sent-2411-047: We set the input to output sequence length to 256\,:\,1 and 256\,:\,256 for discriminative and generative tasks, respectively, catering for edge applications as suggested by Lin et al.~\cite{awq .}
elements.sent-2411-048: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig:mem_footprint , the LLM weights access consumes orders of magnitude larger memory than the activations access.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Although discriminative tasks only need to output a single token (e.g., ``A''/``B''/``C'' for multiple-choice question answering), the weight tensor dimension of an LLM (e.g., 2048 for OPT-1.3B) is much larger than the input token length, leading to memory access dominated by weights.
elements.sent-2411-050: Moreover, generative tasks necessitate repeated weight fetching for every new output token, resulting in significantly higher memory access for LLM weights.
elements.sent-2411-051: Thus, weight quantization is more effective for deploying LLMs in edge scenario where the batch size is small and the input token length is typically short.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/mem_footprint.pdf} \caption{ Total memory access of weights and activations on discriminative tasks (with 256 input tokens and 1 output token) and generative tasks (with 256 input tokens and 256 generated tokens). Note the log scale on the y-axis. Note that the gap between weight and activation memory accesses increases for generative tasks at batch size 1 despite a much larger KV-cache than discriminative tasks. While prior work~\cite{flexgen} has correctly reported a memory bottleneck caused by the KV-cache, this only occurs for 175B+ parameter models with a high batch size (e.g., 512) and a context lengths exceeding 512 tokens. This scenario is less relevant to our focus on low-batch edge LLM inference where the weights indeed dominate the total memory accesses. } \label{fig:mem_footprint}
elements.sent-2411-052: One of the most popular quantization schemes is integer quantization, where a floating-point value is scaled and rounded to a low-precision integer.
elements.sent-2411-053: There are two widely used quantization modes -- symmetric and asymmetric.
elements.sent-2411-054: Symmetric integer quantization can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\Delta = \frac{ W_{f{\text{max}}} }{ 2^{b-1} - 1 } ; \ W_q = Round\left(\frac{ W_f }{ \Delta }\right) ; \ W_{qf} = W_q \cdot \Delta \label{eq:quant_sym}\end{equation} where $W_{f}$ is the original floating-point tensor, $W_{f{\text{max }$ is the absolute maximum value, $b$ is the quantized integer precision, $\Delta$ is the scaling factor, $W_q$ is the quantized integer value, and $W_{qf}$ is the floating-point value after performing dequantization (i.e., re-scaling).}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\Delta = \frac{ W_{f{max}} }{ 2^{b-1} - 1 } ; \ W_q = Round\left(\frac{ W_f }{ \Delta }\right) ; \ W_{qf} = W_q \cdot \Delta \label{eq:quant_sym}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-055: The symmetric quantization assumes that the minimum and maximum values of a tensor have the same absolute value (i.e., symmetric value range), but this is not always true.
elements.sent-2411-056: Hence, another popular mode of quantization is asymmetric quantization, which can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\Delta = \frac{ Range\left(W_f\right) }{ 2^b - 1 } \, ; \ \ z = Round\left(\frac{ -W_{f{\text{min}}} }{ \Delta }\right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}W_q = Round\left(\frac{W_f}{\Delta}\right) + z \, ; \ \ W_{qf} = \left(W_q - z\right) \cdot \Delta \label{eq:quant_asym}\end{equation} where $W_{f{\text{min }$ is the absolute minimum value of $W_{f}$, and $z$ represents the zero-point of the quantized tensor.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: We analyze several techniques that are widely adopted in recent quantization studies, which motivates our proposed BitMoD framework.
elements.sent-2411-058: We mainly focus on weight quantization in our discussion.. Quantization Granularity Matters.
elements.sent-2411-059: Consider a floating-point weight tensor $W_f^{K\times D}$, where $K$ represents the number of output channels and $D$ is the channel size.
elements.sent-2411-060: There are three granularities to quantize the model weight: per-tensor, per-channel, and per-group.
elements.sent-2411-061: The per-tensor quantization uses the same scaling factor to quantize a whole weight tensor, while per-channel quantization divides the weight tensor along the output channel into $K$ vectors, and quantizes every vector $W_f^{1\times D}$ independently.
elements.sent-2411-062: However, given the large tensor size and hidden dimension of LLMs, these two granularities still lead to large quantization error.
elements.sent-2411-063: Specifically, the quantization error of a dequantized weight in Eq. \ref{eq:quant_sym can be expressed as: \begin{equation}Error(W_{qf}) = ErrorRound\left(\frac{ W_f }{ \Delta }\right) \cdot \Delta\end{equation} where ErrorRound is the rounding error during quantization, which has been shown to have an expected value of 0.25~\cite{awq .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Therefore, the quantization error is proportional to the scaling factor $\Delta$, which is further proportional to the maximum value and range for symmetric (Eq.~\ref{eq:quant_sym ) and asymmetric (Eq.~\ref{eq:quant_asym ) quantization, respectively.}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/quant_granularity.pdf} \caption{Maximum value and value range for different quantization granularity. Results are normalized to the standard deviation ($\sigma$) of the weight vector at the corresponding granularity, then averaged across all weight vectors. The per-group granularity has a group size of 128. } \label{fig:quant_granularity}
elements.sent-2411-065: In order to further reduce the quantization error, recent LLM quantization studies adopt the per-group granularity~\cite{group-quant, gptq, awq, students .}
elements.sent-2411-066: The per-group quantization further divides a weight channel $W_f^{1\times D}$ into $D/G$ groups, each with a group size of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-067: The group size introduces extra overhead to store the quantization parameters, i.e., scaling factor (and zero-point) for every group, and is usually set to 128 in SOTA quantization frameworks to balance accuracy and memory overhead~\cite{gptq, awq .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Fig.~\ref{fig:quant_granularity demonstrates the benefits of per-group quantization by showing the maximum value and range in four representative LLMs at different granularity.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The per-group granularity has the lowest maximum value and range, hence will have a lower quantization error compared to the other two granularity.
elements.sent-2411-070: Therefore, we focus on per-group quantization in this work.
elements.sent-2411-072: Numerous studies have proposed custom data types for quantization at the per-channel granularity~\cite{ant, olive, microscaling .}
elements.sent-2411-073: We analyze the effects of adopting different data types for per-channel and per-group weight quantization.
elements.sent-2411-074: We explore four basic data types at 4-bit precision: integer with symmetric (INT4-Sym) and asymmetric (INT4-Asym) quantization, floating-point (FP4), and the Flint data type proposed by ANT~\cite{ant .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Table~\ref{tab:dtype_4b_comp shows the resulting perplexity on the Wikitext-2 dataset \cite{wikitext .}}
elements.sent-2411-077: First, although Flint can achieve better perplexity at the per-channel granularity, it never outperforms other data types at the per-group granularity.
elements.sent-2411-078: Second, the per-group INT4-Asym and FP4 quantization achieve the best perplexity on some but not all studied LLMs, indicating that both asymmetry and FP data types are favorable for per-group quantization.
elements.sent-2411-079: The reason behind this is twofold.
elements.sent-2411-080: First, weight tensors typically exhibit Gaussian-like distribution that fits well to the floating-point data type~\cite{fp6-llm, nf4 .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Second, while the effects of outliers are mitigated by per-group quantization, a weight group can still contain outliers in an asymmetric pattern, being either solely positive or negative, as highlighted in previous studies~\cite{llm-int8, group-quant .}
elements.sent-2411-082: This characteristic benefits from asymmetric quantization.

elements.sent-2411-083: The above observation motivates us to explore new quantization data types that can combine the benefits of asymmetry and FP formats to achieve better accuracy under per-group quantization.
elements.sent-2411-084: We notice that the basic FP data types have symmetric quantization values due to the inherent sign-magnitude binary representation that contains positive and negative zero values.
elements.sent-2411-085: Our key insight is that we can introduce additional asymmetry to FP by repurposing a redundant zero value with another special value.
elements.sent-2411-086: This approach provides us with two key benefits.
elements.sent-2411-087: First, it allows to fully utilize the limited quantization levels.
elements.sent-2411-088: Although the redundant zero value does not affect high-precision formats such as FP16, it constitutes a large fraction of quantization levels at low precision (e.g., $12.5\%$ at 3-bit precision).
elements.sent-2411-089: Second, we can tune the special value to make the extended FP data types better adapt to the per-group weight distribution, which we discuss in Section~\ref{sec:dtype_adapt .. Quantization Bit-width Matters.}
elements.sent-2411-090: While prior LLM accelerators mainly rely on bit-parallel architectures that support 8-bit and 4-bit precision~\cite{ant, olive, figna , recent studies have shown that 6-bit floating-point weights exhibit negligible accuracy loss across various LLM models and tasks~\cite{zeroq_llm, fp6-llm .}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Motivated by this, we analyze the effects of using different 6-bit data types for per-group LLM weight quantization.
elements.sent-2411-092: We consider four data types: integer with symmetric (INT6-Sym) and asymmetric (INT6-Asym) quantization, floating-point with 2-bit exponent and 3-bit mantissa (FP6-E2M3), and floating-point with 3-bit exponent and 2-bit mantissa (FP6-E3M2).
elements.sent-2411-093: Table~\ref{tab:dtype_6b_comp compares the resulting perplexity of different quantization data types on Wikitext-2~\cite{wikitext and C4~\cite{c4 datasets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: On average, the studied 6-bit data types achieve similar and negligible perplexity loss compared to the FP16 baseline.
elements.sent-2411-095: For example, the average perplexity loss of INT6-Sym is less than 0.05, and its simple integer representation offers a promising solution to efficient LLM acceleration.
elements.sent-2411-096: Therefore, it is crucial for an accelerator to support diverse quantization bit-width to offer a better trade-off between memory footprint and model accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-097: A natural solution for accommodating variable precision is to adopt bit-serial architectures~\cite{stripes, pragmatic, bitwave, bbs .}
elements.sent-2411-098: However, existing bit-serial accelerators mainly target the integer data type, which causes significant accuracy loss at 3-bit precision as we will show in Section~\ref{sec:accuracy .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Furthermore, these accelerators cannot leverage per-group quantization for improved accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-100: This is because per-group quantization assigns different scaling factors for different groups, necessitating a floating-point unit with large area overhead to dynamically dequantize the partial sum after computing the dot-product for every group.
elements.sent-2411-101: Thus, an efficient dequantization mechanism with low hardware cost is desirable.

elements.sent-2411-103: Numerous frameworks have been proposed to accelerate LLM execution, as depicted in Table~\ref{tab:overall_comp .}
elements.sent-2411-104: SOTA algorithmic solutions such as AWQ~\cite{awq quantize LLM weights to low-precision integer while preserving high accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Nevertheless, AWQ is optimized for LLM acceleration on GPUs, which lack dedicated mixed-precision computing unit.
elements.sent-2411-106: As a result, it converts the low-precision weights to FP16 and relies on the GPU floating-point pipeline for computation, resulting in poor computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-107: In contrast, ANT~\cite{ant , OliVe~\cite{olive , and FIGNA~\cite{figna propose efficient bit-parallel accelerators for quantized model acceleration.}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: But their precision is limited to 8-bit and 4-bit, which restricts the ability to utilize other precision (e.g., 6-bit) for a better accuracy-efficiency trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-109: Moreover, their accelerators do not natively support per-group quantization, which requires a floating-point unit to dynamically dequantize the per-group partial sum on the fly.
elements.sent-2411-110: While Microscaling~\cite{microscaling accommodates diverse precision, it necessities a floating-point pipeline to handle the shared micro-exponent of a weight group, leading to higher energy consumption compared to other low-precision compute units.}
elements.sent-2411-111: Furthermore, given the significant memory footprint of LLMs, it is desirable to explore sub-4-bit quantization while maintaining good model accuracy, which ANT, OliVe, and Microscaling do not address.
elements.sent-2411-112: As we will show in Section~\ref{sec:accuracy , the custom quantization data types proposed by ANT, OliVe, and Microscaling fail to achieve better accuracy than the simple asymmetric integer quantization at 4-bit weight precision, and cause unacceptable accuracy loss at 3-bit weight precision under per-group quantization.}
elements.sent-2411-113: The above limitation motivates us to propose an efficient LLM acceleration framework that supports a wide range of hardware-friendly bit-widths, while maintaining good accuracy at low weight precision.
elements.sent-2411-114: In this section, we present the BitMoD quantization framework, which includes new data type families tailored for per-group quantization at 3-bit and 4-bit precision.

elements.sent-2411-116: Section~\ref{sec:dtype_adapt presents an enhanced per-group LLM quantization strategy using the proposed data types.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Section~\ref{sec:dequant describes the hardware-efficient per-group dequantization mechanism using integer scaling factors.}
elements.subsection-2411-004: Asymmetric FP3 and FP4 Data Types
elements.sent-2411-118: The basic floating-point formats contain a redundant quantization level due to the sign-magnitude representation that has both $+0$ and $-0$.
elements.sent-2411-119: We propose to replace this redundant zero with another special value to fully utilize the available quantization levels and introduce additional asymmetry.
elements.sent-2411-120: We first use the basic FP3 format to derive our custom 3-bit data type, and then extend our idea to 4-bit precision.

elements.sent-2411-121: The basic FP3 data type contains seven distinct values $\{0, \pm1, \pm2, \pm4\}$.
elements.sent-2411-122: Our main idea is to extend FP3 and allows the redundant zero to be replaced by one of some pre-defined special values.
elements.sent-2411-123: Consequently, a weight group can be quantized by the basic FP3 data type together with a selected special value to minimize the quantization error.
elements.sent-2411-124: Ideally, the special values can have an arbitrary precision.
elements.sent-2411-125: But a high-precision (e.g., FP16) special value leads to more hardware overhead for computing, which offsets the efficiency of low-precision data types.
elements.sent-2411-126: Hence, we limit the special value to low-precision integers.
elements.sent-2411-127: Furthermore, given $N$ as the number of allowed special values, an encoding overhead of $\ceil[\big]{\text{log N}$ bits is needed to specify which special value to be selected during computation.}
elements.sent-2411-128: This selection also requires an $N$-to-1 mux in the hardware implementation.
elements.sent-2411-129: To balance the encoding overhead and hardware complexity, we set $N\,=\,4$ which only requires 2-bit encoding per group.
elements.sent-2411-130: The choice of special values will affect the resulting quantization error because it changes the set of available quantization values.
elements.sent-2411-131: As discussed in Section~\ref{sec:motivation , both asymmetry and floating-point data types are crucial for good accuracy under per-group quantization.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Since the scaling factor and quantized values are ultimately determined by the absolute maximum value of a data type~\cite{t2c , we establish the set of special values based on two principles.}
elements.sent-2411-133: First, some special values should fall inside the numerical range of FP3 to ensure that they do not alter its original absolute maximum (i.e., $4$).
elements.sent-2411-134: This is advantageous for quantizing weight groups exhibiting symmetric, Gaussian-like distribution.
elements.sent-2411-135: Second, some special values could fall outside the numerical range of FP3 to introduce additional asymmetry, i.e., the absolute maximum and minimum quantization values of the extended FP3 are different.
elements.sent-2411-136: This can benefit weight groups that exhibit asymmetric distribution.

elements.sent-2411-179: In this section, we describe the BitMoD hardware design, which leverages the bit-serial computing paradigm to offer a good trade-off between weight precision, model accuracy and hardware efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-180: Section~\ref{sec:bit_serial_term develops a unified bit-serial representation of different low-precision data types supported by BitMoD.}
elements.sent-2411-181: Section~\ref{sec:bitmod_pe details the microarchitecture of the BitMoD processing element (PE).}
elements.sent-2411-182: Section~\ref{sec:bitmod_accel presents the overall accelerator architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-183: Prior studies have demonstrated that per-channel INT8 weight quantization shows no accuracy loss compared to using FP16 weights~\cite{figna, zeroq, smoothquant .}
elements.sent-2411-184: Moreover, as discussed in Section~\ref{sec:motivation , per-group INT6 quantization also has negligible accuracy loss.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Hence, the design target of BitMoD hardware is to support INT8, INT6, as well as the new FP4 and FP3 extensions in a unified architecture.
elements.sent-2411-186: However, the basic values of FP3 and FP4 use the floating-point format, which is not compatible with the integer representation.
elements.sent-2411-187: A naive approach is to convert all data types to INT8, yet this cannot improve the computational efficiency for lower-precision weights.
elements.sent-2411-188: In order to trade-off lower weight precision for improved hardware efficiency, we propose a unified bit-serial representation, where every number is decomposed into a series of bit-serial terms, each containing four parts: sign, exponent (exp), mantissa (man), and bit-significance (bsig).
elements.sent-2411-189: The value of a bit-serial term can be expressed as: \begin{equation}v_{\text{term}} = (-1)^{\text{sign}} \cdot 2^{\text{exp}} \cdot \text{man} \cdot 2^{\text{bsig}} \label{eq:bs_term}\end{equation} Fig.~\ref{fig:bs_dtype describes the bit-serial representation for different data types supported by BitMoD.}
elements.sent-2411-190: For INT8 and INT6, we apply Booth encoding~\cite{booth to decompose their binary strings into four and three 3-bit Booth strings, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-191: The Booth encoding has been widely used in prior bit-serial accelerators to speed up computation~\cite{pragmatic, laconic, fpraker .}
elements.sent-2411-192: Every two adjacent Booth strings have a difference of 2 in bit-significance.
elements.sent-2411-193: The sign, exponent, and mantissa depend on the content of a Booth string, which defines the desired operation when multiplying with another operand $x$.
elements.sent-2411-194: For the extended FP4, we first convert it to fixed-point values in sign-magnitude format with 1 sign bit, 4 integer bits $\{I_3, I_2, I_1, I_0\}$ to handle the largest special value $\pm\,8$ of FP4-EA, and 1 fraction bit $\{F_0\}$ to handle $\pm\,0.5$ and $\pm\,1.5$ of the basic FP4 values.
elements.sent-2411-195: The fixed-point value is then compared with the redundant negative zero.
elements.sent-2411-196: If the comparison result is equal, the negative zero will be replaced by the assigned special value (SV) of a particular weight group.
elements.sent-2411-197: The four allowed special values are stored in a register file (SV\_reg), which only requires one-time programming before deploying an LLM.
elements.sent-2411-198: To obtain the bit-serial term, we observe that all values of the extended FP4 in Table~\ref{tab:fp3_fp4_dtype contain at most two `1' bits after converting to the fixed-point format.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Hence, we use the simple leading-one detector (LOD) to get two bit-serial terms from the first four bits $\{I_3, I_2, I_1, I_0\}$ and last four bits $\{I_2, I_1, I_0, F_0\}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-200: Finally, since the extended FP3 values are a subset of FP4, it can be decoded into two bit-serial terms using the same hardware.
elements.sent-2411-201: Note that the bit-serial decoder is not limited to support the special values shown in Table~\ref{tab:fp3_fp4_dtype .}
elements.sent-2411-202: The special value register file can be programmed with other special values as needed, and the number of decoded bit-serial terms can be minimized with simple modification to the decoder.
elements.sent-2411-203: For example, the special value $7$ can be expressed as two bit-serial terms $2^3$ and $-\,2^0$ instead of using a leading-one detector that emits three bit-serial terms.

elements.sent-2411-204: While BitMoD is able to quantize weight to low precision, activation still remains in FP16.
elements.sent-2411-205: To address this challenge, we propose a mixed-precision bit-serial PE as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:bitmod_pe .}
elements.sent-2411-206: In every cycle, the PE performs a 4-way dot product between four bit-serial weight terms~($w$) and four FP16 activations~($a$).
elements.sent-2411-207: Step~\protect\circled{\small1 first aligns the sum of exponents ($a_e + w_e$) to compute the delta exponent ($\delta_e$).}
elements.sent-2411-208: It also generates the sign ($y_s$) of every product between a weight term and activation.
elements.sent-2411-209: Step~\protect\circled{\small2 performs the bit-serial multiplication between the 1-bit weight mantissa ($w_m$) and 11-bit activation mantissa ($a_m$) including the hidden bit.}
elements.sent-2411-210: The multiplication result is aligned by a right-shifter that is controlled by the delta exponent.
elements.sent-2411-211: We reserve 3 extra bits in the shifter result to account for rounding to the nearest even as suggested by Awad et al.~\cite{fpraker .}
elements.sent-2411-212: The bit-serial dot product of the mantissa is then computed using an adder tree.
elements.sent-2411-213: After producing the bit-serial dot product, Step~\protect\circled{\small3 performs accumulation by first multiplying the dot product with the weight bit-significance ($W_{bsig}$), followed by adding with the accumulator mantissa ($m_{ACC}$).}
elements.sent-2411-214: The accumulated mantissa is then normalized to update the accumulator exponent ($e_{ACC}$).
elements.sent-2411-215: Since BitMoD adopts per-group quantization, the accumulated group partial sum must be dequantized on the fly.
elements.sent-2411-216: To reduce this hardware cost, Step~\protect\circled{\small4 performs dequantization in a bit-serial manner.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Specifically, the accumulator mantissa is multiplied by one bit of the group scaling factor ($\Delta_i$) in every cycle, followed by shift-and-add to obtain the exponent ($e_{GRP}$) and mantissa ($e_{GRP}$) of the dequantized partial sum.
elements.sent-2411-218: One concern of the bit-serial dequantization is whether it will take more cycles than the normal dot product stage and cause potential pipeline stalling.
elements.sent-2411-219: As discussed in Section~\ref{sec:dequant , the per-group scaling factor has 8 bits, which requires 8 cycles for dequantization.}
elements.sent-2411-220: On the other hand, even the lowest-precision data type FP3 in BitMoD requires two cycles to process two bit-serial terms.
elements.sent-2411-221: Given the PE dot-product size of 4 and a commonly used group size of 128, the group dot-product stage takes $128 / 4 \times 2 = 64$ cycles to complete.
elements.sent-2411-222: Therefore, the proposed bit-serial dequantization will never stall the computing pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-223: Furthermore, since INT6 and the extended FP4/FP3 data types contain three and two bit-serial terms, the proposed BitMoD PE is able to compute 4 multiply-accumulate operations in 3 and 2 cycles, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-224: Compared to the normal FP16 multiply-accumulate hardware, BitMoD achieves a throughput improvement of $1.33\times$ and $2\times$ for INT6 and FP4/FP3 data types, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-225: In fact, as will be evaluated in Section~\ref{sec:hardware_eval , the BitMoD PE consumes $24\%$ less area than an FP16 PE, thus is able to provide even higher throughput under an iso-compute area constraint.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Besides matrix multiplication between weights and activations, LLMs also contain the self-attention layer, which involves two matrix multiplication operations between three activation tensors, i.e., query, key, and value.
elements.sent-2411-227: Given that the BitMoD PE only maintains one activation tensor in FP16, the other two activation tensors need to be low-precision integers.
elements.sent-2411-228: Fortunately, prior works have demonstrated that the key and value tensors are very amenable to quantization due to the softmax normalization inside self-attention, and can be safely quantized to INT8 and even INT4 with negligible accuracy loss~\cite{flexgen, atom, smoothquant .}
elements.sent-2411-229: Hence, BitMoD can accommodate the self-attention layer with the proposed bit-serial PE by quantizing the key and value tensors to low-precision integers.
elements.sent-2411-230: Fig. \ref{fig:bitmod_acc shows the overall architecture of the BitMoD accelerator.}
elements.sent-2411-231: The input and weight buffers are banked to provide adequate bandwidth for the access from PEs.
elements.sent-2411-232: The bit-serial term generator receives the weight data and decomposes them into bit-serial terms as discussed in Section~\ref{sec:bit_serial_term .}
elements.sent-2411-233: The main PE array contains $4 \times 4$ tiles connected in a systolic manner.
elements.sent-2411-234: Every PE tile has 8 rows $\times$ 8 columns and adopts an output-stationary dataflow.
elements.sent-2411-235: The bit-serial weight term is broadcast to the whole PE column, while the input is broadcast to the whole PE row.
elements.sent-2411-236: This allows BitMoD to exploit data reuse through both weight-sharing and input-sharing.
elements.sent-2411-237: Every PE column contains a local output buffer and an accumulator, which is used to accumulate the per-group partial sum from a PE to obtain the final per-channel output activation.
elements.sent-2411-238: Since processing a weight group takes many cycles, there is enough time to drain the whole PE column using only one shared accumulator to amortize the hardware cost.
elements.sent-2411-239: For evaluation, we choose six representative LLMs with diverse model sizes, including \text{OPT-1.3B ~\cite{opt , \text{Phi-2B ~\cite{phi-2b , \text{Yi-6B ~\cite{yi-6b , \text{Llama-2-7B , \text{Llama-2-13B ~\cite{llama-2 , and \text{Llama-3-8B ~\cite{llama-3 .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-240: We obtain the pre-trained models from the HuggingFace repository, and implement the proposed BitMoD quantization framework in PyTorch.
elements.sent-2411-241: To evaluate the effects of quantization on the resulting model performance, we consider both discriminative and generative tasks.
elements.sent-2411-242: For discriminative tasks, we evaluate three benchmarks: HellaSwag~\cite{hellaswag , WinoGrande~\cite{winogrande , and Piqa~\cite{piqa under the zero-shot setting using LM-Evaluation-Harness~\cite{lm-eval .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-243: For generative tasks, we choose Wikitext-2~\cite{wikitext and C4~\cite{c4 datasets and evaluate the perplexity following the methodology in prior quantization works~\cite{gptq, awq, quarot, omniquant .. Quantization Data Types.}}}
elements.sent-2411-244: We compare the model accuracy of BitMoD with four baseline quantization data types:.
elements.sent-2411-245: ANT~\cite{ant , which adaptively uses different data types to quantize different tensors at the per-channel granularity.}
elements.sent-2411-246: OliVe~\cite{olive , that introduces an outlier-victim pair encoding mechanism, which sacrifices the normal value (i.e., victim) adjacent to the outlier to accommodate the important outlier value.}
elements.sent-2411-247: Microscaling (MX)~\cite{microscaling , which groups 32 low-precision FP weights with an extra 8-bit shared exponent.}
elements.sent-2411-248: Per-group asymmetric integer quantization, which is commonly adopted in prior software quantization methods.
elements.sent-2411-249: To ensure a fair comparison with BitMoD, we only apply the data types of ANT, OliVe, and MX to LLM weight quantization while maintaining activations in FP16.
elements.sent-2411-250: Despite that the original ANT and OliVe frameworks only support per-channel quantization due to the absence of dedicated dequantization hardware, we have extended their algorithms for per-group quantization.
elements.sent-2411-251: This allows to compare the model accuracy purely based on the employed quantization data types.
elements.sent-2411-252: While we mainly focus on weight quantization in our evaluation, Section~\ref{sec:comb_sota_quant demonstrates that BitMoD can be combined with SOTA activation quantization scheme, which offers the potential to reduce activation memory as well.}

elements.sent-2411-253: In addition to co-design works, we show that BitMoD can be seamlessly integrated into existing software-only quantization optimizations to further reduce the memory footprint or achieve better model performance.
elements.sent-2411-254: We combine BitMoD with three software quantization methods:.
elements.sent-2411-255: SmoothQuant~\cite{smoothquant , which targets both weight and activation quantization in INT8.}
elements.sent-2411-256: It addresses the quantization difficulty of large activation magnitude by partially migrating it to weights.
elements.sent-2411-257: AWQ~\cite{awq , which employs activation-aware weight quantization to protect the salient weight channels corresponding to larger activation magnitudes.}
elements.sent-2411-258: OmniQuant~\cite{omniquant , which modulates the outlier weight values by optimizing the clipping threshold through block-wise fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-259: To integrate BitMoD with these software-only methods, we replace their original weight quantizers that use integer data types with the extended FP4 and FP3 data types of BitMoD.. Accelerator Baselines.

elements.sent-2411-278: Moreover, on average, BitMoD achieves a big improvement of $2.2\%$ in model accuracy compared to the asymmetric integer quantization that is widely used in SOTA software quantization methods.
elements.sent-2411-280: As discussed in Section~\ref{sec:asym_fp_type , BitMoD introduces new data types by adding extra resolution and asymmetry to FP3 and FP4.}
elements.sent-2411-281: We analyze the effects of these different data types on the perplexity of three studied Llama models.
elements.sent-2411-282: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:dtype_ablation , the BitMoD data types with both extra resolution and asymmetry achieve the best perplexity.}
elements.sent-2411-283: Compared to the basic FP4 data type, FP4-ER shows a greater improvement in perplexity than FP4-EA.
elements.sent-2411-284: This is because at 4-bit, the basic FP4 still has enough quantization levels to quantize a weight group with asymmetric distribution, and adding extra resolution can better reduce the quantization error.
elements.sent-2411-285: On the contrary, for 3-bit precision, FP3-EA achieves much better perplexity than FP3-ER.
elements.sent-2411-286: Given fewer quantization levels at 3-bit, the extra asymmetry introduced by FP3-EA has a larger impact when accounting for weight groups with asymmetric distribution.

elements.sent-2411-300: The unified bit-serial representation allows BitMoD to support different weight data types with low hardware cost.
elements.sent-2411-301: As a result, the BitMoD PE is $24\%$ smaller than the baseline PE, which allows to fit more BitMoD PEs within the same compute area.

elements.sent-2411-303: Fig. \ref{fig:speedup presents the hardware performance normalized to the baseline FP16 accelerator for discriminative and generative tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-304: BitMoD achieves the best performance under both lossless and lossy quantization.
elements.sent-2411-305: The performance gain of BitMoD mainly comes from its careful algorithm-hardware co-design of different quantization data types.
elements.sent-2411-306: Since discriminative tasks are compute-bound and mainly involve matrix-matrix multiplications, the higher throughput of BitMoD PE leads to better performance than the baseline PE.
elements.sent-2411-307: In contrast, OliVe requires more complicated PEs with significant hardware overhead to accommodate the outliers, which have a much wider numerical range (e.g., $\{24, ..., 192\}$ at 4-bit precision).
elements.sent-2411-308: In comparison, the BitMoD data type has a small value range and can be efficiently processed with the proposed unified bit-serial representation.
elements.sent-2411-309: Regarding memory-bound generative tasks, BitMoD can quantize LLM weights to very low precision such as 3-bit, which offers significant memory saving while maintaining good perplexity.
elements.sent-2411-310: On the contrary, ANT and OliVe do not natively support per-group quantization due to the lack of dedicated dequantization hardware, and cannot maintain acceptable model quality under per-channel quantization using 3-bit weight precision.
elements.sent-2411-311: Therefore, they must adopt a higher weight precision to compensate for the significant degradation in perplexity.
elements.sent-2411-312: Overall, the lossless BitMoD achieves $1.99\times$ and $2.41\times$ speedup for discriminative and generative tasks, respectively compared to the baseline FP16 architecture.
elements.sent-2411-313: The lossy BitMoD achieves $1.72\times$\;/\;$1.56\times$ and $1.66\times$\;/\;$1.39\times$ speedup for discriminative and generative tasks, respectively compared to ANT\;/\;OliVe.

elements.sent-2411-320: The proposed BitMoD can offer good trade-offs between model accuracy and hardware efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-321: To demonstrate this, we analyze the relationship between energy-delay product (EDP) and model perplexity of Phi-2B and Llama-2-7B on Wikitext-2.

elements.sent-2411-324: Note that while the 5-bit precision is not presented explicitly, BitMoD can be easily extended to perform bit-serial INT5 computation using its Booth encoder.
elements.sent-2411-325: Similarly, the custom data types introduced by ANT and OliVe can be extended to 5-bit precision based on their data type definition.
elements.sent-2411-326: As indicated in Fig. \ref{fig:pareto , the lower left region indicates a better trade-off between perplexity and EDP.}
elements.sent-2411-327: Although ANT and OliVe propose different algorithm-hardware co-design approaches for LLM acceleration, they only leverage the per-channel quantization granularity that fails to preserve the model quality at very low precision, and they lack a unified architecture to efficiently support different data types and precision.
elements.sent-2411-328: In contrast, BitMoD exploits new data types tailored for per-group quantization and adopts an efficient bit-serial computing paradigm to support various data types.
elements.sent-2411-329: Hence, BitMoD can always sit on the Pareto frontier.
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/bp_bs_area_power.pdf} \caption{Normalized area and power of BitMoD and different bit-parallel PEs.} \label{fig:bp_bs_comp}
elements.sent-2411-330: FIGNA~\cite{figna proposes a family of bit-parallel PEs for arithmetic between low-precision integer weight and floating-point activation.}
elements.sent-2411-331: However, every FIGNA PE is designed separately and only supports one weight precision, which fails to offer a trade-off between model accuracy and hardware efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-332: We explore the possibility of FIGNA to support mixed-precision integer weights.
elements.sent-2411-333: We consider a baseline FIGNA-like PE that performs multiply-accumulate between FP16 activation and INT8 weight.
elements.sent-2411-334: We extend the baseline PE to support either one FP16-INT8 operation or two FP16-INT4 operations, which multiply the same FP16 activation with two INT4 weights.
elements.sent-2411-335: Fig.~\ref{fig:bp_bs_comp compares the normalized area and power of different PEs.}
elements.sent-2411-336: Although the FP-INT8 PE has the smallest area, adding support for mixed weight precision incurs significant hardware overhead and leads to even higher area and power consumption than the conventional FP-FP PE.
elements.sent-2411-337: This is because a bit-parallel PE computing two FP16-INT4 operations will produce two separate outputs, doubling the cost of a floating-point accumulator and output register.
elements.sent-2411-338: In contrast, the bit-serial BitMoD PE trades-off between weight precision and latency, which requires only one accumulator and output register for any weight precision.
elements.sent-2411-339: Consequently, BitMoD offers the highest flexibility to support variable weight precision with better efficiency compared to the decomposable bit-parallel PE.

elements.sent-2411-357: There is an abundance of prior work on DNN accelerators~\cite{stripes, pragmatic, laconic, fpraker, bitwave, bbs, olaccel, gobo, mokey, ant, olive, cambricon-s, sparten, dstc, eureka, highlight, microscaling, figna .}
elements.sent-2411-358: These accelerators propose specialized processing elements and data flow to match the computational characteristics and memory access pattern of DNNs.
elements.sent-2411-359: Some accelerators exploit value sparsity to accelerate small-scale DNNs with the help of retraining~\cite{cambricon-s, sparten, dstc, eureka, highlight .}
elements.sent-2411-360: Other works target low-precision DNN acceleration based on model quantization~\cite{olaccel, gobo, mokey, ant, olive, microscaling, figna .}
elements.sent-2411-361: Among them, \cite{ant, olive, microscaling introduce custom data types to better fit the value distribution of DNNs.}
elements.sent-2411-362: Another line of works relies on bit-serial computing to scale the performance with lower operand precision, and leverages bit-level sparsity to skip ineffectual bit operations~\cite{stripes, pragmatic, laconic, fpraker, bitwave, bbs .}
elements.sent-2411-363: The proposed BitMoD combines the benefits of quantization and bit-serial computing to efficiently trade-off between weight precision and hardware efficiency.. LLM Quantization.
elements.sent-2411-364: Numerous algorithmic studies have proposed quantization solutions to reduce the memory footprint of LLMs~\cite{gptq, awq, llm-int8, students, llm-qat, zeroq_llm, smoothquant, quarot, omniquant, efficientqat .}
elements.sent-2411-365: Most of these works rely on asymmetric integer quantization for LLM weights while applying other techniques to optimize the quantization parameters.
elements.sent-2411-366: The proposed BitMoD data type is orthogonal to many of these works and can be synergistically combined with different quantization optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-367: In this paper, we introduce BitMoD, an algorithm-hardware co-design scheme for efficient LLM acceleration.
elements.sent-2411-368: On the algorithm side, BitMoD designs new data types that are tailored for per-group LLM weight quantization at very low precision.
elements.sent-2411-369: By intelligently repurposing the redundant zero value to an additional number, BitMoD extends the resolution or range of 3-bit-and 4-bit floating-point data types.
elements.sent-2411-370: Moreover, the BitMoD quantization framework can be seamlessly integrated with existing software-only quantization methods to further improve the model performance.
elements.sent-2411-371: On the hardware side, BitMoD proposes a unified bit-serial representation for diverse low-precision data types and an efficient bit-serial PE to process quantized weight and FP16 activation.
elements.sent-2411-372: Our evaluation demonstrates that BitMoD significantly outperforms existing LLM quantization methods, pushing the limit of LLM weight quantization to a new state-of-the-art.
elements.sent-2411-373: Compared to prior accelerators ANT\,/\,OliVe, BitMoD achieves $1.69\times$\;/\;$1.48\times$ speedup and $1.48\times$\;/\;$1.31\times$ better energy efficiency, while being able to support diverse weight precision to offer a good trade-off between model accuracy and hardware efficiency.

elements.sent-2411-400: Please go to every sub-folder and refer to the corresponding `README.md' for detailed setup instructions.
elements.sent-2411-401: Note that AWQ, OmniQuant, and SmoothQuant will require different conda environments.
elements.sent-2411-402: Hence, please change back to the base environment after installing a conda environment before creating the next.
elements.sent-2411-403: For example, the AWQ environment can be created by running:.
elements.lstlisting-2411-002: [language=bash] $ cd AWQ-BitMoD $ conda create -n awq-bitmod python=3.10 -y $ conda activate awq-bitmod # Follow `README.md' inside AWQ-BitMoD folder to set up other dependencies. $ conda deactivate # change back to the base environment
elements.sent-2411-404: The OmniQuant and SmoothQuant environments can be created in a similar way by repeating the above step and following their `README.md' to set up other dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-405: Once the environment is set up, we will conduct five sets of experiments, each corresponding to one of the five sub-folders within the BitMoD-HPCA-25 repository.
